name: Wardley Mapping
origin_domain: technology strategy, business strategy, competitive intelligence
year_developed: 2005 at Fotango (Simon Wardley's company); refined and expanded 2008-2010 at Canonical UK; continued evolution through Wardley's blog (blog.gardeviance.org, later medium.com/wardleymaps) and community contributions; book 'Wardley Mapping, The Knowledge' by Mark Craddock published 2021
progenitor: Simon Wardley — British researcher, former CEO of Fotango, and strategist at Canonical UK. Wardley developed the mapping technique from frustrated personal experience: 'I knew Sun Tzu's five factors, I knew about the importance of the landscape... but I didn't actually know how to map it.' He created the method to operationalize strategic situational awareness, heavily influenced by Sun Tzu's 'The Art of War' and the concept of topographical intelligence in business.
driving_insight: The central insight is that everything evolves through predictable stages of commoditization — from genesis (unique, chaotic) through custom-built (differentiating) to product (competitive market) to commodity (utility). This evolution is a force of nature, like gravity — it happens whether you plan for it or not. Strategy is fundamentally about understanding WHERE each component of your value chain sits on this evolution axis and acting accordingly (build in genesis, buy at commodity, exploit at product). The map reveals: (1) what you should build vs. buy; (2) where competitors are vulnerable; (3) where the market is heading; (4) what assumptions about your business are becoming obsolete. Most strategic errors come from applying the wrong methods to a component's evolution stage (e.g., trying to standardize something still in genesis, or custom-building something that is already a commodity). The map makes situational awareness explicit and shareable — you cannot play chess without seeing the board.
decision_style: deliberative and emergent — the map provides topographical intelligence that informs decisions, but decisions themselves are human judgment calls. The map reveals: which components are strategic (genesis/custom-built, high value) vs. commoditized (low margin, utility); where inertia barriers exist; where competitors are positioned; where the market is moving. Decisions emerge from reading the landscape, not from algorithmic optimization. The methodology provides a common visual language for strategic debate — multiple stakeholders (CTO, CFO, product managers) can point to the same map and argue from shared situational awareness. Consensus emerges from seeing the same landscape. The approach combines deliberative analysis (mapping the current state) with emergent decision-making (what to do about it emerges from the map's revelations).
problem_class_targeted: technology strategy and business positioning problems in dynamic, evolving markets — problems where 'what should we build vs. buy, and when?' is the central question. Designed for: medium-to-large organizations navigating technology evolution; competitive strategy (where to attack, where to defend); build-vs-buy decisions; identifying commoditization and disruption threats; organizational transformation planning. Well-suited for: dynamic landscapes where components evolve at different rates; situations requiring cross-functional strategic alignment. Less suited for: purely operational problems with no strategic dimension; problems in static, unchanging environments; problems where the value chain is trivial (one or two components). The methodology assumes the problem landscape evolves — if nothing is changing, a map adds less value.
ordered_phases: The full Strategy Cycle uses an OODA-loop-inspired sequence: (1) PURPOSE — articulate the why: moral imperative, scope of the organization, and strategic direction; (2) LANDSCAPE — map the competitive environment: identify the user, their needs, the value chain of components required to meet those needs, and each component's position on the evolution axis (Genesis -> Custom -> Product -> Commodity); (3) CLIMATE — analyze the patterns and forces acting on the landscape: anticipate how components will evolve; identify competitors' positions and likely moves; apply climatic patterns (everything evolves, no one size fits all, efficiency enables innovation, past success breeds inertia); (4) DOCTRINE — apply universal strategic principles applicable regardless of landscape: focus on user needs, use a bias for data, think fast/inexpensive/restrained/elegant (FIRE), be transparent, use appropriate methods for each evolution stage; (5) LEADERSHIP — make the strategic decisions: where to attack, where to defend, what to build vs. buy, how to organize; this is the 'Decide' step that translates map reading into action. The cycle is iterative — maps are updated continuously as components evolve. This is an Observe-Orient-Decide-Act (OODA) cycle adapted for business strategy.
feedback_loops: Feedback loops are explicit and modeled on the OODA loop: (1) The Landscape phase produces a map; (2) The Climate phase reads the map and anticipates evolution; (3) Leadership decisions are implemented; (4) As components evolve and competitors move, the Landscape changes, triggering re-mapping; (5) The entire Strategy Cycle is a continuous feedback loop — faster OODA cycling is a competitive advantage (you adapt faster than competitors); (6) Within the map, component movement (left to right along the evolution axis) is inherent — commoditization is a one-way ratchet that continuously reshapes the landscape; (7) The map exposes assumptions that can be tested — 'we assume this component will stay in custom-built for 3 years' becomes a monitorable hypothesis. Loops are explicit and designed — the cyclical nature of the Strategy Cycle is central to the methodology. Faster, more accurate cycles win.
gates: No formal approval gates — Wardley Mapping is a strategic intelligence tool, not a project management framework. In practice, informal checks occur at: (1) Map validity — does the map accurately represent the value chain? Stakeholders challenge component placement and missing dependencies; (2) Strategic decision — leadership must commit to the actions derived from map reading (attack here, buy there); (3) Implementation review — are the actions producing the expected landscape shifts? If not, the map is updated and new decisions are made. There is no tollgate process, no certification, and no formal gatekeeper. The methodology is deliberately anti-bureaucratic — the pain of mapping (argument, challenge, refinement) is the validation mechanism. When consensus cannot be reached on the map, it reveals a gap in shared understanding that must be resolved through discussion and evidence. The map is never 'approved' — it is continuously debated and refined.
exit_criteria: No formal 'done' state — Wardley Mapping is a continuous strategic practice. A mapping exercise is 'complete enough' when: (1) The map captures the user needs, value chain, and component evolution positions with sufficient accuracy to inform strategic decisions; (2) Leadership has reached consensus on the strategic moves indicated by the map; (3) An action plan exists. However, the map must be updated as the landscape evolves — components move, competitors act, new technologies emerge. The exit criterion for a specific strategic cycle is that decisions are made and implementation is underway. But mapping itself never ends — it is ongoing situational awareness. Partial completion: a map can be useful even when incomplete — a rough map is better than no map. The methodology encourages 'good enough for the decision at hand' rather than perfection.
decomposition_method: Decomposition is value-chain-driven and user-need-anchored: (1) Start from the user and their needs (top of the value chain); (2) For each need, identify the components required to satisfy it — components are activities, practices, data, knowledge, or technology; (3) For each component, identify what sub-components it depends on — recursively decompose down the value chain; (4) Each component is positioned on the evolution axis (Genesis, Custom-Built, Product, Commodity); (5) Decomposition is anchored by user visibility — components higher in the chain are more visible to users; components lower are invisible infrastructure; (6) This is a domain-driven, dependency-driven decomposition — what does this component need to exist? — rather than a task-driven decomposition. The value chain structure reveals dependencies (Component A needs Component B, which needs Component C). The decomposition stops when: (a) a component is a commodity utility that can simply be purchased; (b) further decomposition would not change strategic decisions; (c) the component is outside the organization's scope of interest.
granularity_guidance: Granularity is governed by strategic relevance: (1) A useful map typically contains 15-40 components for a single value chain; (2) Decompose until each component can be meaningfully placed on the evolution axis — if you can't decide whether a component is genesis or custom-built, it may need further decomposition; (3) Components that are at the same evolution stage and share the same strategic treatment can be grouped; (4) A component is 'leaf-level' when it can be assigned a clear evolution stage AND a clear strategic treatment (build, buy, outsource, exploit); (5) The stopping rule: decompose until the map reveals actionable strategic insights — further granularity beyond this point adds complexity without changing decisions; (6) There is no 8-80 hour rule equivalent — the map is a strategic artifact, not a work plan. Granularity is driven by the decisions the map needs to inform, not by execution planning needs.
boundary_rules: Boundary rules are user-need-anchored: (1) The map's scope is defined by a specific user and their needs — everything required to satisfy those needs is in scope; (2) The vertical chain is bounded at the top by the user need and at the bottom by commodities (components so standard they are treated as utilities); (3) Components are linked by dependency relationships — if A needs B to function, B is part of A's value chain; (4) Overlap between maps is managed by scope — different maps for different users/needs; (5) MECE is not strictly enforced — the same component may appear in multiple maps because the same infrastructure can support multiple user needs; (6) The map is a model, not a specification — clean boundaries are desirable but not always achievable; (7) The Inertia barrier concept provides a boundary check — components to the right of the inertia barrier face resistance to change from existing practices and sunk costs. Boundary disputes are resolved by debating whether a component is essential to meeting the user's need.
risk_assessment_mechanism: Risk assessment is landscape-driven rather than checklist-driven: (1) Evolution risk — components in Genesis are high-risk (unpredictable, unproven); components in Commodity are low-risk (stable, standardized); (2) Inertia risk — components near inertia barriers risk being disrupted by more evolved alternatives; (3) Competitor positioning risk — components where competitors have superior positions represent vulnerability; (4) Dependency chain risk — a weak component deep in the value chain can compromise everything above it; (5) Assumption risk — strategic assumptions about evolution speed and competitor moves are explicit on the map and can be challenged; (6) The map reveals risk by making the landscape visible — risks that are invisible in spreadsheets or reports become obvious when mapped; (7) Risk identification is pattern-matching (against known climatic patterns) and human-judgment-based — there is no formal risk scoring, FMEA, or probabilistic analysis. The primary risk mechanism is 'seeing the board' — most strategic failures come from not seeing the threat until it's too late.
unknown_handling: When encountering unknowns: (1) Mark them on the map — 'here be dragons.' Unknown components are placed with question marks, making ignorance visible; (2) Use the map to identify what specifically is unknown — is the component's existence unknown (is it in the value chain?), its evolution stage unknown (where is it?), or its trajectory unknown (how fast is it moving?); (3) Probe — conduct research, talk to experts, run experiments to reduce the unknowns; (4) Use climatic patterns to anticipate — 'everything evolves' means today's custom will be tomorrow's product; (5) Default-safe — do not make irreversible strategic commitments in areas of high uncertainty; (6) The map frames unknowns as hypotheses to be tested, not as blockers to action. Unknowns at the commodity end of the chain are less concerning (you can buy multiple options) than unknowns at the genesis end (you are betting on an unproven technology). The methodology handles unknowns by making them visible and scoped, enabling risk-calibrated action.
default_stance_on_unknown: PERMISSIVE with caution — the methodology encourages action despite uncertainty because waiting for perfect information means being outpaced by competitors. However, the type of action matters: (1) Genesis-stage unknowns: experiment, explore, probe — small bets, not big commitments; (2) Product-stage unknowns: competitive analysis, market research — moderate investment to resolve unknowns; (3) Commodity-stage unknowns: low-risk — you can switch suppliers, so unknowns are tolerable; (4) The FIRE principle (Fast, Inexpensive, Restrained, Elegant) applies — act quickly but with limited exposure; (5) The map reduces the cost of being wrong — you know what you're uncertain about and can monitor it. The default is 'act with bounded risk, learn, and adapt' rather than 'block until certain.' This is a deliberate contrast with methodologies like DMAIC that default restrictive — Wardley Mapping acknowledges that in competitive strategy, the cost of inaction often exceeds the cost of being wrong.
safety_guardrails: Safety mechanisms are strategic rather than technical: (1) The FIRE principle (Fast, Inexpensive, Restrained, Elegant) bounds the blast radius of failed strategic moves — small experiments, not big bets; (2) The evolution axis provides a natural guardrail — applying commodity methods to genesis components is flagged as a doctrinal error (and vice versa); (3) The map makes assumptions visible — 'we assume this component will take 3 years to reach product' is explicit and monitorable; (4) Climatic patterns (e.g., 'past success breeds inertia') warn against common strategic errors; (5) Doctrine provides universal principles applicable regardless of landscape; (6) For LLM/agent contexts: no I/O firewalls, sandboxing, or kill switches exist — the methodology was designed for human strategic reasoning, not autonomous execution; (7) The primary safety mechanism is shared situational awareness — a team looking at the same map catches each other's blind spots.
human_decision_points: Humans are fully in the loop at all stages. Specific human decision points: (1) User and scope definition — which user, which need?; (2) Value chain identification — what components are required?; (3) Evolution positioning — where is each component on the genesis-to-commodity axis?; (4) Climatic pattern recognition — what patterns are in play?; (5) Competitor positioning — where are competitors on this map?; (6) Strategic decision — where to attack, where to defend, what to build, what to buy?; (7) Organizational design — how should teams be structured relative to the map?; (8) Assumption challenging — which assumptions need testing? The methodology is a tool for human strategic reasoning — it cannot function without human judgment. Humans are IN the loop — they create the map, read the map, and make the decisions. The map amplifies human intelligence rather than replacing it.
override_capability: Yes, at multiple levels: (1) Any stakeholder can challenge any component's position on the map — 'this should be in product, not custom-built'; (2) Leadership can override strategic decisions derived from the map — the map informs but does not command; (3) The map is continuously revisable — anyone can propose updates as new information emerges; (4) Override granularity: per-component (challenge a single component's placement or existence), per-chain (challenge the value chain structure), per-map (challenge the scope/user definition). The methodology encourages challenge — 'argument is a feature, not a bug, of mapping.' The map improves through contested refinement. There is no automated process to override because there is no automated process — all mapping is human-created and human-maintained.
fallback_when_no_human: Wardley Mapping cannot function without human strategic judgment. If the strategist is unavailable: (1) The map remains as a static snapshot of the landscape at the time it was created; (2) No updates occur as components evolve and competitors move — the map becomes increasingly outdated; (3) No new strategic decisions are made — the organization operates on outdated assumptions; (4) The map degrades in value over time as the gap between the mapped landscape and the actual landscape widens. In an LLM-agent context: an AI could generate plausible maps and evolution projections from available data, and could identify climatic patterns. But the strategic decisions (where to attack, what to bet on) require understanding of organizational capabilities, risk appetite, and market timing that current LLMs cannot reliably provide. The methodology degrades from a strategic intelligence tool to a documentation artifact without human engagement.
max_problem_size: Demonstrated at national infrastructure scale — Wardley Mapping has been used by UK Government Digital Service (GDS) to map entire government digital services; HS2 (UK high-speed rail) for infrastructure planning; multiple global corporations for enterprise IT strategy. Practical limits: (1) A single map typically covers one value chain with 15-40 components — larger scopes are split into multiple linked maps; (2) The UK government has mapped hundreds of interconnected services using the methodology; (3) What breaks at scale: map complexity — a map with 100+ components becomes unreadable; (4) Cognitive load on strategists — the value of the map is in the discussion it generates, and discussions become unwieldy with too many components; (5) Coordination across maps — when multiple maps share components (same infrastructure serving multiple services), keeping them synchronized becomes challenging. The theoretical limit is set by the ability to maintain a coherent, discussable map — practically, this is bounded at ~50-70 components per map before splitting is necessary.
parallel_execution: Yes, parallelism is natural: (1) Multiple maps can be developed in parallel for different users/needs; (2) Multiple teams can use the same map in parallel to derive different strategic insights (one team analyzes build-vs-buy, another analyzes organizational structure); (3) Different components within a map can be analyzed in parallel by different strategists; (4) Dependency resolution across maps is managed through shared components — if two maps share a component, the component's evolution stage must be consistent; (5) Parallelism is first-class — the methodology is designed for organizational use where multiple strategists work simultaneously; (6) The landscape itself is parallel — components evolve simultaneously and independently. Within a single map, the Strategy Cycle is sequential (Purpose -> Landscape -> Climate -> Doctrine -> Leadership) but multiple cycles run in parallel across different maps.
team_scaling_model: Sub-linear scaling with diminishing returns: (1) A mapping exercise works best with 5-12 participants representing diverse perspectives (technology, finance, operations, product, executive); (2) Fewer than 5 risks groupthink and blind spots; (3) More than 12 becomes unwieldy — the discussion fragments, and maintaining a shared mental model becomes difficult; (4) Scaling beyond a single team is achieved by having multiple teams create and maintain their own maps while sharing common components — this is the 'federated mapping' model; (5) One skilled facilitator/mapper can guide ~8-12 participants; (6) Training more mappers enables more parallel mapping exercises; (7) Negative scaling occurs when too many participants try to contribute to a single map — the exercise becomes a political negotiation rather than an analytical exercise. The optimal team is cross-functional, diverse, and small enough for productive debate.
cost_scaling_model: Cost is almost entirely human time — the methodology requires no specialized software (pen and paper or simple drawing tools suffice). Cost scaling: (1) Initial mapping exercise: 1-3 days for a focused workshop (5-12 people); (2) Map maintenance: 2-4 hours per month per map to update component positions; (3) Strategic review cycles: 1-2 days quarterly; (4) Training investment: 1-2 days to learn the basics, months-to-years to develop strategic reading fluency; (5) Costs scale linearly with the number of maps and mapping exercises; (6) For LLM-based map generation: cost would be minimal — an LLM can generate a plausible value chain and evolution positions from a prompt; (7) The primary cost is the opportunity cost of strategic thinking time — this is intentional, as the thinking is the value, not a cost to be minimized. The methodology is deliberately low-cost — Wardley designed it to be accessible with minimal tools because 'strategy is too important to be gated by expensive consultants.'
replanning_built_in: Replanning is inherent and first-class — the OODA-loop-inspired Strategy Cycle is explicitly designed for continuous replanning. Key aspects: (1) The map is continuously updated as components evolve and competitors move — replanning is triggered by landscape change, not by a calendar; (2) Replanning is both local (updating individual component positions) and global (rethinking the entire value chain when a disruption occurs); (3) The OODA loop framework (Observe, Orient, Decide, Act) makes replanning the default, not the exception — you are always observing and re-orienting; (4) Faster OODA cycles are a competitive advantage — the methodology rewards organizations that replan quickly and accurately; (5) Replanning is proactive — you anticipate evolution and adjust strategy ahead of competitors, not just react to surprises. The methodology treats strategy as a continuous adaptive process, not a periodic planning event.
trigger_mechanism: Adaptation is triggered by: (1) Component evolution — a component visibly shifts from custom to product, changing its strategic treatment; (2) Competitor movement — a competitor enters a space, builds a better position, or commoditizes a previously differentiating component; (3) New component emergence — a new technology or practice appears in the value chain (typically at genesis or custom); (4) Assumption violation — a strategic assumption about evolution speed or competitor behavior proves wrong; (5) Climatic pattern activation — a recognized pattern (e.g., inertia, efficiency enabling innovation) manifests; (6) Regular review — scheduled strategic reviews where the map is re-examined. There are no automated triggers — recognition depends on human observation and interpretation of market signals. The map makes triggers visible — a component that was in custom is now clearly in product, and the map flags the mismatch between position and method.
adaptation_cost: Low to moderate: (1) Local updates (changing one component's position) take minutes to hours — reposition and assess cascading implications; (2) Value chain restructuring (adding/removing components, rethinking dependencies) takes hours to a day of workshop time; (3) Strategic reorientation (changing the entire strategic direction based on a fundamentally changed landscape) takes days to weeks but this is normal strategic work, not a special replanning cost; (4) State preservation is high — the previous map is retained as a snapshot showing how the landscape looked at a point in time; (5) Lost work is minimal — previous analysis becomes historical context rather than wasted effort; (6) The low adaptation cost is intentional — if updating the map were expensive, organizations would do it less often, and their strategic awareness would degrade. The methodology's core value proposition is enabling frequent, low-cost strategic adaptation.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Recovery is adaptive rather than procedural: (1) Detection — strategic failure is detected when outcomes diverge from the map's predictions (a component evolved faster or slower than expected, a competitor moved unexpectedly); (2) Isolation — trace the failure to specific map assumptions: was the component position wrong? was a climatic pattern misread? was a competitor not on the map?; (3) Recovery — update the map with corrected positions and assumptions, re-run the Strategy Cycle, and make new strategic decisions; (4) Blast radius containment — strategic decisions informed by the map should follow the FIRE principle (Fast, Inexpensive, Restrained, Elegant), limiting the cost of being wrong; (5) Graceful degradation — a partially wrong map is still more useful than no map; strategic failures expose which parts of the map need correction; (6) The OODA loop framework normalizes failure — being wrong is expected, what matters is how fast you detect it and adapt. There is no formal retry mechanism — strategic adaptation is continuous, not episodic. The methodology treats failure as a signal to update the map, not a reason to abandon mapping.
output_artifacts: Primary artifact: the Wardley Map itself — a two-dimensional diagram with the value chain on the y-axis (user needs at top, commodities at bottom) and evolution on the x-axis (genesis at left, commodity at right). Supporting artifacts: (1) Annotated map with strategic notes (attack here, defend this, buy this, build this); (2) Evolution trajectory projections — expected movement of key components over time; (3) Competitor maps — the same landscape with competitor positions overlaid; (4) Doctrine checklist — verification that universal principles are being followed; (5) Strategic narrative — written explanation of the landscape, the chosen moves, and the rationale; (6) Assumption log — explicit list of strategic assumptions underpinning the map; (7) Gameplay patterns — documented competitive moves (e.g., open approaches, exploiting inertia, undermining barriers). The map is the central artifact — all other documents elaborate on what the map shows.
briefing_format: The map IS the briefing — it is a visual compression of the strategic landscape designed to be read and discussed in a group setting. A standard strategic briefing involves: (1) Display the map; (2) Walk the value chain from user need down to commodities; (3) Explain why each component is positioned where it is; (4) Highlight key climatic patterns (inertia barriers, evolution pressures); (5) Show competitor positions; (6) Present the chosen strategic moves with map annotations; (7) Open discussion — the map invites challenge. The briefing is conversational and visual — the map serves as the shared reference point. For compressed communication, a single annotated map conveys the full strategic picture. The format is stakeholder-appropriate — executives get the high-level moves; implementation teams get the component-level implications. No standard template exists because each map is unique to its landscape, but the visual grammar is consistent (y-axis: value chain; x-axis: evolution).
traceability: Moderate and visual: (1) Every strategic decision is traceable to the map — 'we are building X because it is in Genesis and strategically critical' or 'we are buying Y because it is a commodity'; (2) Component positioning can be traced to evidence — market research, expert interviews, competitor analysis; (3) The assumption log provides traceability from conclusions back to underlying assumptions; (4) Previous map versions provide a historical record — you can see how the landscape was understood at each point in time; (5) However, traceability is informal — there is no formal decision record, no audit trail, no version control beyond what the team maintains; (6) Rationale is preserved in the strategic narrative and in team memory — the discussion that produced the map is the rationale. The methodology trusts that the map itself makes the logic visible — if you can see the landscape, you can understand the strategy. Formal traceability is not a priority — speed of strategic adaptation is.
agent_communication_protocol: Not applicable — Wardley Mapping is a human strategic methodology. In an LLM-agent adaptation: (1) A Wardley Map could be serialized as structured data — a graph with nodes (components with name, evolution_stage, visibility_level) and edges (dependency relationships); (2) Multiple agents could share maps using this serialization — one agent generates a map, another critiques component positioning, a third derives strategic recommendations; (3) MCP or A2A could carry map-structured messages; (4) Agent discovery would need a shared component taxonomy to ensure consistent naming across agents; (5) Sync communication for map review and debate; async for landscape monitoring and updates. Custom protocol design would be required — no existing standard maps to Wardley Mapping's dual-axis structure.
learning_memory_architecture: Wardley Mapping has an implicit but underdeveloped learning architecture: (1) Semantic memory — the climatic patterns, doctrine, and gameplay patterns are a reusable semantic knowledge base that improves with the practitioner's experience; (2) Episodic memory — each map is an episodic record of a landscape at a point in time; experienced mappers accumulate a library of maps across industries; (3) Procedural memory — the skill of mapping (knowing what to include, where to place components, how to read the landscape) is procedural knowledge developed through practice; (4) Community learning — the Wardley Mapping community (online forums, conferences, blogs) serves as a distributed learning system where maps are shared and critiqued; (5) No formal post-hoc distillation — the methodology has no built-in mechanism to extract patterns from completed maps; (6) For LLM adaptation: a corpus of annotated maps with strategic outcomes would be valuable training data; the climatic patterns are already well-documented and available for few-shot prompting. The most transferable memory structure is the pattern library (climate, doctrine, gameplay).
latency_bounds: Batch — Wardley Mapping is a strategic thinking methodology with no real-time requirements. Typical timeline: (1) Initial map creation: 1-3 days of workshop effort; (2) Map refinement and consensus: 1-4 weeks of iterative discussion; (3) Strategic decision-making: days to weeks after the map stabilizes; (4) Map review cycles: quarterly or when triggered by landscape changes; (5) Map updates: hours per quarter. Under time pressure: the methodology degrades — rushed maps misplace components and miss critical dependencies. However, a rough map produced in hours is still better than no map — 'a bad map is better than no map, because at least you can see what's wrong with it.' The minimum useful mapping exercise is approximately 4 hours for a focused team with strong domain knowledge. The methodology cannot be used for real-time tactical decisions — it is designed for strategic tempo (weeks-to-months).
evaluation_benchmarking: No formal benchmarks exist. Evaluation is practical and organizational: (1) Strategic decision quality — did the map lead to better build-vs-buy, attack-vs-defend decisions?; (2) Anticipation accuracy — did the map correctly anticipate component evolution and competitor moves?; (3) Organizational alignment — did the map create shared understanding and reduce strategic disagreement?; (4) Competitive outcomes — did organizations using Wardley Mapping outperform competitors in the same landscape?; (5) Adoption as proxy — UK Government (GDS), HS2, multiple corporations have adopted the methodology. Validation is primarily through practitioner testimony and organizational adoption rather than controlled experiments. No SWE-bench or academic benchmark equivalents exist. For LLM-generated maps: evaluation would require expert mappers to assess component placement accuracy, dependency completeness, and strategic insight quality.
mapping_strength: Strong mapping to LLM agent workflows. What transfers directly: (1) Value chain decomposition maps naturally to LLM reasoning — 'what components does the user need to accomplish X?'; (2) Evolution stage assessment maps to LLM knowledge — LLMs can assess whether a technology is genesis, custom, product, or commodity based on training data; (3) Climatic pattern recognition maps to LLM pattern-matching capability; (4) Doctrine application maps to rule-based reasoning; (5) The structured visual grammar (y-axis: visibility, x-axis: evolution) is learnable and reproducible by LLMs; (6) Strategic recommendation generation (build vs. buy, attack vs. defend) maps to LLM reasoning about trade-offs. What needs translation: (1) The map is inherently visual — LLMs need text-based representations (JSON graph, text descriptions); (2) Component evolution forecasting requires market data LLMs may not have; (3) Competitor positioning requires intelligence gathering; (4) The strategic judgment (which moves to make) requires understanding of organizational capabilities and risk appetite. Overall: the mapping and analysis are highly LLM-compatible; the strategic decision-making requires human/organizational context.
gaps: What is missing for software/LLM agent contexts: (1) No automated landscape monitoring — the methodology relies on humans to notice when components evolve or competitors move; (2) No formal component taxonomy — components are named ad-hoc, making it difficult for agents to share and merge maps; (3) No computational evolution model — evolution projections are human judgment, not quantitative forecasting; (4) No built-in decision optimization — the methodology provides situational awareness but does not prescribe specific moves (which is intentional but limits automation); (5) No integration with execution systems — the map informs strategy but has no automated link to implementation; (6) No mechanism for handling unknown components — 'here be dragons' is a placeholder, not a resolution mechanism; (7) The methodology assumes a human strategic community with shared context — replicating this with LLM agents requires explicit knowledge sharing protocols that don't exist yet; (8) No safety mechanisms for autonomous strategic action — the methodology was designed for human deliberation, not autonomous execution.
adaptation_required: To apply Wardley Mapping to the JORDAN pipeline: (1) Use Wardley Mapping in the RESEARCH phase — generate a landscape map of the problem domain showing the value chain of components, their evolution stages, and relevant climatic patterns; (2) Use the map to inform the PLAN phase — strategic decisions (where to intervene, what approach to use) should be explicitly grounded in the landscape; (3) Add a 'Strategic Context' component to the PLAN node — before proposing a solution, the agent should map the solution landscape and justify why the chosen approach fits the evolution stage; (4) Incorporate 'build vs. buy' analysis — for software components, should the agent write code from scratch (genesis/custom) or use existing libraries/services (product/commodity)?; (5) Add climatic pattern checks to RISK_ASSESSMENT — check for inertia, competitor positioning, and evolution risks; (6) The map becomes a living artifact updated as the agent learns more about the problem domain. The key design recommendation: Wardley Mapping should inform the 'where to play' and 'how to approach' decisions in the PLAN node, providing strategic context that purely task-oriented planning lacks.
validation_context: Industry adoption: UK Government Digital Service (GDS) used Wardley Mapping for government digital transformation strategy; HS2 (UK high-speed rail) used it for infrastructure technology planning; multiple commercial organizations in technology, finance, and telecommunications. Community: active online community at medium.com/wardleymaps; annual Map Camp conferences; learnwardleymapping.com educational resources. Consulting: used by strategic consultancies for technology strategy engagements. The methodology has been validated primarily through practitioner adoption and reported strategic outcomes rather than formal academic studies. Simon Wardley documented the methodology's development and application extensively on his blog over 15+ years, providing a rich case-study literature. The methodology is open — no certification, no licensing — which has driven broad adoption but limits formal validation data.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) Map worship — treating the map as reality rather than a model; making decisions based on the map without validating against real-world evidence; (2) Positioning wars — teams spend more time arguing about component positions than making decisions; the map becomes an end rather than a means; (3) False precision — placing components at exact points on the evolution axis when the underlying evidence is vague; the map creates an illusion of certainty; (4) Static mapping — creating a map once and never updating it; the map becomes a historical artifact rather than a living strategic tool; (5) Missing components — failing to include critical dependencies in the value chain because they are 'invisible' (taken for granted); the strategy is blindsided when an invisible component fails or is disrupted; (6) User-need anchoring failure — building a map around organizational structure rather than user needs; the map reinforces existing silos rather than revealing user-centered value chains; (7) Competitor blindness — mapping your own landscape without mapping competitors; your strategy is based on a view of the board that doesn't show the other players; (8) Evolution speed errors — assuming components will evolve faster or slower than they actually do; being too early (betting on genesis that stays genesis for years) or too late (being disrupted by a commoditization you didn't see coming); (9) Method mismatch — the map correctly identifies a component as commodity, but the organization continues to custom-build it because 'that's how we've always done it'; (10) Over-application — using Wardley Mapping for problems where a simpler tool would suffice; the mapping overhead exceeds the strategic benefit. Known counterexample: Wardley Mapping provides less value in perfectly competitive commodity markets where all components are commodities and no strategic differentiation is possible.
where_still_used: UK Government: GDS, Cabinet Office, various departments for digital and technology strategy; Infrastructure: HS2, Crossrail, Transport for London; Technology companies: various startups and scale-ups using mapping for product and platform strategy; Consulting: multiple strategic consultancies offer Wardley Mapping as a service; Defense and security: used for cybersecurity strategy and defense technology planning; Open source / community: active online community, Map Camp conferences (London, Boston, Berlin), online mapping tools (OnlineWardleyMaps, MapScript); Education: taught in executive education (University of Cambridge executive programs), DevOps and platform engineering conferences, and through community workshops. The methodology is gaining adoption as a complement to Domain-Driven Design, Team Topologies, and platform engineering in technology organizations.
